lesson.text: Three buckets, and the boundary between them is where the real work is.

GDPR sorts data into three tiers. Not Personal Data cannot identify a natural person and sits outside GDPR's scope entirely. Personal Data (Art. 4(1)) directly or indirectly identifies someone. Special Category Data (Art. 9/10) — health, ethnicity, religion, political opinion, and similar — identifies someone AND reveals something about them that demands heightened protection.

Pseudonymised data is still personal data. A hospital record labeled only "Patient #K9R21" looks anonymous, but if the hospital holds a registry mapping that code back to a name, re-identification is possible — and Recital 26 says that's enough to keep it inside GDPR.

Special-category data doesn't have to be stated outright to count. An ad platform that tags a user "interested in left-wing politics" purely from browsing and click patterns — never a word the user typed — is still processing political-opinion data under Art. 9. The EDPB has confirmed that inferred sensitive data is still sensitive data.

The opposite mistake also happens: "78% ICU bed occupancy this week" looks like it's about patients, but it's an aggregate figure with no link back to any identifiable individual — not personal data at all, regardless of how sensitive the underlying topic (health) sounds.

quiz.q: A hospital's patient portal refers to a record only as "Patient #K9R21" — the real name is kept in a separate secure registry accessible only to records staff. Is this personal data under GDPR?
choices: No — it's anonymised, so GDPR doesn't apply
Yes — pseudonymised data is still personal data
Only once the patient's name is also shown on screen
explain: Anonymisation means re-identification is impossible even for the controller. Here the hospital holds the mapping table — that's pseudonymisation, not anonymisation.
Correct — Recital 26 GDPR treats pseudonymised data as personal data whenever the controller can reasonably re-identify it, which the hospital clearly can via its own registry.
The identifying link already exists the moment the mapping table does — showing the name on screen isn't what makes it personal data, it already was.

quiz.q: An ad platform automatically tags a user as "interested in left-wing politics" based on browsing and ad-click patterns — the user never stated a political view. How should this be classified?
choices: Not personal data — it's inferred, not stated by the user
Personal data, but not special category
explain: Being algorithmically inferred doesn't put it outside GDPR — it's still information tied to an identifiable user.
Political opinion is one of the categories Art. 9(1) lists explicitly — inference doesn't downgrade it.
Correct — the EDPB has confirmed inferred sensitive data is still sensitive data. Data revealing political opinions is Art. 9 special-category data whether stated or inferred.

quiz.q: A hospital's weekly management report states "78% bed occupancy in ICU this week, 62% in Paediatrics." Is this personal data?
choices: Yes — it describes what's happening to patients
No — it's an aggregate figure with no link to any identifiable individual
Only if the hospital is small enough that patients could be guessed
explain: It's about hospital operations in aggregate, not any one patient's record — nothing here identifies a person.
Correct — aggregate statistics with no link to an identifiable individual fall outside GDPR's definition of personal data, however sensitive the underlying topic sounds.
Small-sample re-identification risk is a real DPIA consideration elsewhere, but a bare occupancy percentage like this one isn't reasoning about individuals at all.
